<link rel="stylesheet" media="print" href="./css/print.css">
<link rel="stylesheet" href="./css/theme-light.css">
<script src="./js/theme-toggle.js"></script>
<script src="./concepts/bundle.js" defer></script>
<script src="./js/topic-hotkeys.js" defer></script>
<script src="./js/topic-lineage.js" defer></script>
<!-- breadcrumb-head-auto-end -->
<script src="./js/progress.js"></script>
<script src="./js/quiz.js"></script>
<script src="./js/katex-select.js"></script>
<script src="./quizzes/bundle.js"></script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  table.cayley{border-collapse:collapse;margin:.5rem auto}
  table.cayley td{border:1px solid var(--line);padding:.25rem .45rem;text-align:center;
    min-width:2.2em;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.88rem;cursor:pointer;user-select:none}
  table.cayley td.hdr{background:#0b0f16;color:var(--yellow);font-weight:600;cursor:default}
  table.cayley td.identity-row,table.cayley td.identity-col{background:#1a1f2c}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}

  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>
<!-- display-prefs-head-auto-begin -->
<script src="./js/display-prefs.js"></script>
<!-- display-prefs-head-auto-end -->
</head>
rawBodyPrefix:
← Notebook
1 Structure 2 Groups / Cayley 3 Dihedral Dn 4 ℤ/nℤ 5 Polynomial gcd 6 Finite fields 7 Ideals & quotient rings 8 Field extensions 9 Cosets & Lagrange 10 Quotients & iso theorems 11 Orbits & Burnside 12 Sylow 13 Finite abelian 14 Polynomial rings 15 Semidirect & SES

Abstract algebra

Groups, rings, fields — the three ways a set can carry "arithmetic," with one interactive widget per section to build intuition.

blocks.html:
1. One, two, three operations

The structures of algebra are what you get when you demand that a set comes equipped with one or two operations that behave like familiar arithmetic. You relax different axioms to get different structures:

group one associative operation, an identity, and inverses. Models symmetry: the invertible transformations of anything.

ring two operations — addition (abelian group) and multiplication (associative, distributive). Models arithmetic like $\mathbb{Z}$, $\mathbb{Z}[x]$, matrices.

field a ring where multiplication is also a group on nonzero elements. Models numbers — places you can solve linear equations. $\mathbb{Q},\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C}$, and, crucially, the finite fields $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$.

Everything below is a tour through examples where you can actually poke the axioms.

Used in
Abstract algebra · Cayley tables and subgroups
Commutative algebra · Ideals and quotient rings
Galois theory · Field extensions and automorphisms
Galois theory · Solvability by radicals
Lie groups · Lie groups and matrix examples
Probability theory · Markov chains

2. Groups via Cayley tables

The cleanest way to see a finite group is its multiplication table. Rows indexed by $g$, columns by $h$, cell $(g,h)$ contains $g\cdot h$. Closure means every cell is an element of $G$; the identity gives a row and column that are exact copies of the header; each row/column is a permutation of $G$ (this is how you cheat to read off Cayley's theorem).

Cayley table explorer
pick a group · pick a subgroup · see cosets

Normal subgroup detector. A subgroup $H\leq G$ is normal iff left cosets equal right cosets, i.e. $gH=Hg$ for all $g$. Visually: the coset coloring is the same whether you look at the rows of $H$ or the columns of $H$. When $H\trianglelefteq G$ you can form the quotient $G/H$.

Used in
Abstract algebra · Quotients and finite fields
Abstract algebra · Subgroups, cosets, and Lagrange
Representation theory · Group representations

3. Dihedral groups Dn

The symmetries of a regular $n$-gon form the dihedral group $D_n$ of order $2n$. It has the presentation

$D_n = \langle r, s \mid r^n = s^2 = e,\; srs = r^{-1}\rangle$.

Any element is uniquely $r^a s^b$ with $0\leq a

Dn — apply r and s
build words · watch the polygon
5
Under the hood: each button-press pre-multiplies the current element. The relation $srs=r^{-1}$ collapses any word to a canonical $r^a s^b$.

4. The ring ℤ/nℤ

Reducing integers mod $n$ makes $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$ a commutative ring of size $n$. Its structure hinges on a single number-theoretic fact: $a\in(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\times$ iff $\gcd(a,n)=1$.

When $n=p$ is prime, every nonzero element is a unit and $\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}=\mathbb{F}_p$ is a field. When $n$ is composite, the nonzero non-units are zero divisors: there's some $b\ne0$ with $ab\equiv 0\pmod n$.

ℤ/nℤ clock
green = unit · red = zero divisor
12
3 4
</section>

5. Polynomial rings & the Euclidean algorithm

For a field $k$, the ring $k[x]$ is a Euclidean domain with "size" = degree. So the same Euclidean algorithm that computes $\gcd$ in $\mathbb{Z}$ computes $\gcd$ in $k[x]$ — and with it, Bézout coefficients, irreducibility tests via divisibility, and field extensions like $\mathbb{Q}(\alpha)=\mathbb{Q}[x]/(m_\alpha)$.

gcd in k[x]
watch long division step by step
presets:

Key consequence. In $k[x]$, $(f)+(g)=(\gcd(f,g))$, so every ideal is principal. This is why $k[x]/(p)$ is a field precisely when $p$ is irreducible — a fact we'll lean on in the next section to build $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$.

6. Finite fields 𝔽pⁿ

For every prime power $q=p^n$ there is a unique field with exactly $q$ elements, up to isomorphism. It's built as

$\mathbb{F}_{p^n} = \mathbb{F}_p[x]/(m(x))$

for any irreducible $m\in\mathbb{F}_p[x]$ of degree $n$. Elements are polynomials in $\alpha$ (a root of $m$) of degree $

Two facts make these fields beautiful: (i) the multiplicative group $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}^\times$ is cyclic of order $p^n-1$, so there's always a primitive element $g$ with every nonzero element equal to some $g^i$; (ii) the Frobenius $\phi(x)=x^p$ is an automorphism, and the full Galois group $\operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{F}_{p^n}/\mathbb{F}_p)$ is cyclic of order $n$, generated by $\phi$.

Explore 𝔽pⁿ
click an element

Frobenius orbits partition $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ into Galois-conjugate classes; each orbit of size $d\mid n$ is the roots of some irreducible $m_d\in\mathbb{F}_p[x]$ of degree $d$. Summing orbit sizes gives the classical identity $p^n = \sum_{d\mid n} d\cdot N_d$ where $N_d$ is the number of monic irreducibles of degree $d$ in $\mathbb{F}_p[x]$.

Used in
Abstract algebra · Ideals and quotient rings
Category theory · Yoneda, limits, and adjunctions
Galois theory · Normal and separable extensions
Galois theory · Solvability by radicals

7. Ideals and quotient rings

For commutative rings, the analogue of a normal subgroup is an ideal: a subset $I\subseteq R$ closed under addition and under multiplication by any ring element, i.e. $rI\subseteq I$ for every $r\in R$. Ideals are exactly the kernels of ring homomorphisms, and the quotient ring $R/I$ is well-defined with the usual coset arithmetic $(r+I)+(s+I)=r+s+I,\ (r+I)(s+I)=rs+I$.

Two sub-species matter most. A prime ideal $\mathfrak{p}$ satisfies $ab\in\mathfrak{p}\Rightarrow a\in\mathfrak{p}$ or $b\in\mathfrak{p}$, equivalently $R/\mathfrak{p}$ is an integral domain. A maximal ideal $\mathfrak{m}$ is a proper ideal with no proper ideal strictly containing it, equivalently $R/\mathfrak{m}$ is a field. Concretely in $\mathbb{Z}$ the prime ideals are $(0)$ and $(p)$ for $p$ prime, and the maximal ideals are exactly $(p)$; this is why $\mathbb{Z}/(p)=\mathbb{F}_p$ is a field while $\mathbb{Z}/(6)$ is not even a domain (since $2\cdot 3\equiv 0$).

The Third Isomorphism Theorem for rings, $R/I \big/ (J/I)\cong R/J$ whenever $I\subseteq J$, shows that passing to a quotient just collapses ideals above $I$ onto the corresponding ideals of $R/I$. This is the algebraic counterpart of restricting to a closed subscheme in algebraic geometry.

Used in
Abstract algebra · Field extensions and minimal polynomials
Abstract algebra · Polynomial rings and irreducibility

8. Field extensions and minimal polynomials

A field extension $F\subseteq K$ turns $K$ into an $F$-vector space; its dimension $[K:F]$ is the degree of the extension. An element $\alpha\in K$ is algebraic over $F$ if $p(\alpha)=0$ for some nonzero $p\in F[x]$, and transcendental otherwise. For algebraic $\alpha$, the set of polynomials killing $\alpha$ is an ideal of $F[x]$ — and since $F[x]$ is a principal ideal domain, that ideal is $(m_\alpha)$ for a unique monic irreducible $m_\alpha$, the minimal polynomial.

Adjoining $\alpha$ gives $F(\alpha)\cong F[x]/(m_\alpha)$ by the evaluation map $x\mapsto \alpha$ and the First Isomorphism Theorem: the kernel is exactly $(m_\alpha)$, so the image — a subring of $K$ — is isomorphic to the quotient, which is a field because $(m_\alpha)$ is maximal whenever $m_\alpha$ is irreducible. Its dimension over $F$ is $\deg m_\alpha$, so $[F(\alpha):F]=\deg m_\alpha$. The Tower Law $[L:F]=[L:K]\cdot[K:F]$ then multiplies degrees through a chain $F\subseteq K\subseteq L$, e.g. $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3}):\mathbb{Q}]=2\cdot 2=4$.

A polynomial $f\in F[x]$ of degree $n$ splits in an extension $K/F$ if $f=c\prod_{i=1}^n(x-\alpha_i)$ with each $\alpha_i\in K$; the smallest such $K$ is the splitting field of $f$, always algebraic and of finite degree $\le n!$. Splitting fields are the springboard for Galois theory, where automorphisms of $K$ fixing $F$ permute the roots of $f$.

Used in
Galois theory · Solvability by radicals
Projective plane · The cross-ratio

9. Subgroups, cosets, and Lagrange

Pick a subgroup $H\leq G$. Left multiplication by $g\in G$ is a bijection $H\to gH$, so every left coset has size exactly $|H|$. Distinct cosets are disjoint and together they cover $G$, so the coset partition chops $G$ into blocks of equal size. The number of blocks is the index $[G:H]=|G|/|H|$, and this immediately gives Lagrange's theorem: $|H|$ divides $|G|$.

Right cosets $Hg$ give a parallel partition. In general left- and right-coset partitions disagree. The subgroup $H$ is normal precisely when they agree — equivalently, $gHg^{-1}=H$ for all $g$, or $H$ is the kernel of some homomorphism out of $G$. That is exactly the condition that $G/H$ inherits a group structure in §10.

Coset partition visualizer
pick G, pick H · compare left vs. right cosets

Used in
Abstract algebra · Group actions, orbits, and Burnside
Abstract algebra · Quotient groups and isomorphism theorems
Abstract algebra · Sylow theorems

10. Quotient groups and isomorphism theorems

When $N\trianglelefteq G$, the set of cosets $G/N$ carries a well-defined multiplication $(aN)(bN)=(ab)N$ — well-defined precisely because $N$ is normal, so the choice of representatives doesn't matter. The map $\pi:G\to G/N$, $g\mapsto gN$, is a surjective homomorphism with kernel $N$, and every surjection of $G$ arises this way.

The three isomorphism theorems then organize homomorphism arithmetic:

I $G/\ker\varphi\cong\mathrm{im}\,\varphi$ for any homomorphism $\varphi:G\to H$.

II For $H\leq G$ and $N\trianglelefteq G$: $HN/N\cong H/(H\cap N)$.

III For $N\trianglelefteq M\trianglelefteq G$ with $N\trianglelefteq G$: $(G/N)/(M/N)\cong G/M$.

Quotient builder
pick G, pick normal N · see G/N as a table

Used in
Abstract algebra · Finite abelian groups
Abstract algebra · Semidirect products and short exact sequences

11. Group actions, orbits, and Burnside

A (left) action of $G$ on a set $X$ is a map $G\times X\to X$ satisfying $e\cdot x=x$ and $(gh)\cdot x=g\cdot(h\cdot x)$. The action partitions $X$ into orbits $G\cdot x=\{g\cdot x: g\in G\}$. For each $x$, its stabilizer $\mathrm{Stab}(x)=\{g: g\cdot x=x\}$ is a subgroup of $G$, and the orbit-stabilizer theorem gives

$|G\cdot x|=[G:\mathrm{Stab}(x)]=|G|/|\mathrm{Stab}(x)|$.

The Burnside/Cauchy–Frobenius lemma counts orbits as an average:

$\#\{\text{orbits}\}=\dfrac{1}{|G|}\sum_{g\in G}|\mathrm{Fix}(g)|$.

Below, $D_4$ acts on the $2^4=16$ colorings of a $2\times 2$ grid by rotating/reflecting the grid. Click a symmetry $g\in D_4$ to highlight the colorings it fixes and watch the Burnside sum assemble.

Orbit / Burnside explorer
D₄ acting on 2×2 two-colored grid · click a group element

Used in
Abstract algebra · Semidirect products and short exact sequences
Abstract algebra · Sylow theorems

12. Sylow theorems

Write $|G|=p^k m$ with $\gcd(p,m)=1$. A Sylow $p$-subgroup is a subgroup of order exactly $p^k$. The three Sylow theorems say:

I Sylow $p$-subgroups exist for every prime $p\mid |G|$.

II Any two Sylow $p$-subgroups are conjugate; in particular any $p$-subgroup of $G$ is contained in some Sylow $p$-subgroup.

III The number $n_p$ of Sylow $p$-subgroups divides $m$ and satisfies $n_p\equiv 1\pmod p$. When $n_p=1$, the unique Sylow $p$-subgroup is normal.

The $n_p=1$ case is the main lever: it forces existence of a normal subgroup and often pins down $G$ up to isomorphism (semidirect product with the other Sylow subgroup).

Sylow calculator
enter n ≤ 200 · see allowed n_p for each prime dividing n
</section>

13. Finite abelian groups

The fundamental theorem of finite abelian groups says every finite abelian group $A$ is (uniquely up to reordering) a direct sum of cyclic groups of prime-power order:

$A\cong\bigoplus_{i}\mathbb{Z}/p_i^{a_i}\mathbb{Z}.$

Equivalently, $A\cong\mathbb{Z}/d_1\oplus\cdots\oplus\mathbb{Z}/d_r$ with $d_1\mid d_2\mid\cdots\mid d_r$ (invariant factors). To count abelian groups of order $n=\prod p^{v_p(n)}$, fix each prime $p\mid n$ independently: the $p$-primary part is a direct sum of cyclic groups whose exponents partition $v_p(n)$. So the count is $\prod_{p\mid n} P(v_p(n))$, where $P(k)$ is the number of integer partitions of $k$.

Abelian group decomposer
enter n · list all abelian groups of order n
</section>

14. Polynomial rings and irreducibility

Over a field $k$, the ring $k[x]$ is a Euclidean domain: its irreducibles are the prime elements, and every polynomial factors uniquely (up to units) into irreducibles. Over $\mathbb{Z}$, Gauss's lemma says a primitive polynomial in $\mathbb{Z}[x]$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{Z}$ iff it is irreducible over $\mathbb{Q}$.

Two practical tests for a polynomial $f(x)=a_n x^n+\cdots+a_0\in\mathbb{Z}[x]$:

rational-root every rational root is $\pm p/q$ with $p\mid a_0$ and $q\mid a_n$; testing the finite list either finds a root (reducible) or rules out degree-$1$ factors.

Eisenstein if some prime $p$ satisfies $p\nmid a_n$, $p\mid a_i$ for all $i

Irreducibility tester
enter coefficients (highest degree first)
presets:
</section>

15. Semidirect products and short exact sequences

A short exact sequence of groups is a chain $1\to N\xrightarrow{\iota} G\xrightarrow{\pi} H\to 1$ with $\iota$ injective, $\pi$ surjective, and $\mathrm{im}\,\iota=\ker\pi$. So $N\trianglelefteq G$ and $G/N\cong H$. The sequence splits when $\pi$ admits a homomorphism section $s:H\to G$; equivalently $G$ is isomorphic to a semidirect product $N\rtimes_\varphi H$, where $\varphi:H\to\mathrm{Aut}(N)$, $\varphi(h)(n)=s(h)\,n\,s(h)^{-1}$.

Multiplication in $N\rtimes_\varphi H$ is $(n_1,h_1)(n_2,h_2)=(n_1\varphi_{h_1}(n_2),\,h_1 h_2)$. When $\varphi$ is the trivial homomorphism, this collapses to the direct product $N\times H$. For a nontrivial twist, the resulting $G$ is non-abelian (assuming $N$ is).

Below, fix $N=\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$ and $H=\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}$, and toggle $\varphi$ between the trivial map and inversion $a\mapsto -a$. The two choices reproduce $\mathbb{Z}/n\times\mathbb{Z}/2$ and $D_n$ respectively.

SES classifier
1 → ℤ/n → G → ℤ/2 → 1 · pick the twist φ
4
</section>
